relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Jacob went on, and divine messengers met him on the way.
He recognized the place as a divine camp and named it Mahanaim.
Jacob sent messengers ahead toward Esau in Edom.
He lowered himself in speech, calling Esau lord and himself servant.
He sent word of his abundance, seeking favor in Esau’s eyes.
The answer returned: Esau was coming with four hundred men.
Fear split Jacob’s camp in two.
He planned for survival if one camp was struck.
Jacob prayed to the Divine of Abraham and Isaac, holding God to the promise of return.
He confessed smallness before covenant-kindness and truth: once only a staff, now two camps.
He asked to be rescued from Esau’s hand.
He remembered the seed promise as sand beyond counting.
That night he prepared a gift for Esau.
Goats and sheep became part of the offering of peace.
Camels, cattle, and donkeys filled out the gift.
He arranged the droves with space between them.
Each servant was prepared for Esau’s questions.
Each drove would speak humility and gift before Jacob’s face appeared.
The message was repeated through every drove.
Jacob hoped the gift would cover Esau’s face, so that Esau might lift Jacob’s face.
The gift crossed before him while he remained in the camp.
At night Jacob sent his family across the Jabbok.
He sent across all that belonged to him.
Left alone, Jacob wrestled until dawn.
The struggle wounded him in the place of walking.
Jacob refused release without blessing.
He was asked his name and had to answer: Jacob.
The heel-grasper became Israel, the one who struggles with the Divine and humans and prevails.
Jacob asked for the name, but received blessing instead.
He named the place Peniel, because he had seen the Divine face to face and lived.
The sun rose on him as he limped forward.
The wound became memory for the children of Israel.
